---
title: "NTK-Mirror: Forward-Pass Behavioral Control of Frozen Language Models"
subtitle: "A reverse-engineered technical report on the scientific method behind `ntkmirror` v0.4.0"
author: "Technical report prepared from source-code analysis of the `ntkmirror` repository (Hassana Labs / Leon Chlon)"
date: 2026-06-11
abstract: |
  `ntkmirror` adapts frozen Hugging Face causal language models without
  changing a single weight. Its deployable object is a sparse *signed
  log-gate controller*: a few thousand scalar gates that multiplicatively
  rescale selected decoder-layer channels, $h' = e^{s} h$ with
  $|s| \le s_{\max}$. Around this mechanism the repository builds a complete
  research program: a falsifiable theory of when gate updates can imitate
  full-weight SGD steps (the activation-control NTK dual), an algebra for
  composing controllers in log-gate space, a versioned persistent memory of
  controllers with retrieval, an order-sensitivity hallucination verifier
  (ISR), a serving runtime with admission policies, and a benchmark protocol
  whose controls are designed to refute its own headline claims. This report
  reverse-engineers that scientific method from the code and documentation.
  It describes what the system measures and how each claim can fail; the
  repository ships methodology and invariant tests, not committed empirical
  results, and this report quotes no numbers that the code does not define.
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    code-overflow: wrap
execute:
  enabled: false
---

# Executive summary

`ntkmirror` (v0.4.0, Python ≥ 3.10, `torch` ≥ 2.3, `transformers` ≥ 4.43)
is a LoRA-free fine-tuning package with an unusual property: **the base model
is never modified**. There are no adapter modules, no merged weights, and no
permanent edits. Adaptation lives entirely in a sparse set of scalar gates
attached by removable PyTorch forward hooks:

$$
h'_{\ell,t,c} \;=\; e^{\,s_{\ell,c}}\, h_{\ell,t,c},
\qquad |s_{\ell,c}| \le s_{\max},
$$

where $\ell$ indexes a decoder layer, $c$ a residual-stream channel, $t$ a
token position, and $s_{\max}$ (`max_log_gate`, default $0.05$) bounds every
gate. A controller is therefore a small, serializable, *detachable* artifact
— closer to a configuration than to a checkpoint.

That one design decision generates the entire system. Because the
intervention is multiplicative in activation space, it is **additive in
log-gate space**, so controllers compose by weighted addition
(@sec-composition). Because controllers are small files bound to a model
revision, they can be stored, versioned, retrieved, and composed on demand
as a **persistent behavioral memory** (@sec-memory). Because gates form a
low-dimensional control basis on a frozen network, one can ask a sharp
theoretical question — *can a gate update realize the same projected-logit
displacement as a full-weight SGD step?* — and answer it with a measurable
residual rather than an assumption (@sec-dual). And because attachment is
request-scoped and reversible, serving reduces to hook hygiene plus artifact
admission (@sec-runtime).

The subsystem inventory:

| Subsystem | Module(s) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Signed log-gate controller | `controller.py`, `layers.py`, `losses.py`, `data.py` | Select, train, save, load, attach sparse gates |
| NTK dual diagnostics | `dual.py` | Test whether full-weight SGD fields lie in the gate tangent range |
| Composition algebra | `compose.py` | Add controllers in log-gate space; pre-flight interference checks |
| Persistent memory | `memory.py`, `retrieval.py` | Versioned controller store with lexical/embedding/hybrid retrieval |
| ISR hallucination verifier | `isr.py` | Order-sensitivity score for evidence-support verification |
| Runtime & governance | `runtime.py`, `artifacts.py` | Request-scoped application, admission policy, lint/card/doctor |
| CLI | `cli.py` | One command per instrument (`fit`, `secant`, `dual-diagnose`, `isr-auc`, …) |
| Benchmark protocol | `scripts/benchmark_persistent_memory_suite.py` | Memory benchmark with built-in refutation controls |

The thesis of this report is that the repository is best read not as a
feature list but as a **sequence of falsifiable claims, each paired with an
instrument that can refute it**. Each of the core methodology documents in
`docs/` carries a section titled "Failure modes …" or "Failure hypotheses
…";
every headline mechanism ships with a diagnostic command whose output can
contradict the mechanism's advertised story; and the test suite encodes the
refutation thresholds as standing CI assertions. @sec-method reconstructs
this structure explicitly as a hypothesis ledger.

::: {.callout-important}
## What this report does *not* contain

The repository ships methodology, instruments, and invariant tests — **no
committed empirical results**. Accordingly, this report describes what the
system measures and how each measurement can fail. It does not report
accuracies, AUROCs, or speedups, because none are recorded in the source
tree. Where the documentation cites observed behavior (e.g., the diffusion
appendix's gate-collapse statistics), that provenance is flagged explicitly.
:::

# System overview and architecture {#sec-overview}

## Design constraints

Three constraints, stated or implied throughout the code, shape every
component:

1. **Frozen weights.** `ForwardFineTuner.__init__` calls
   `_freeze(model)` immediately; the only `nn.Parameter` the package ever
   creates is the gate vector `raw`. A dedicated test
   (`test_controller_does_not_register_base_layers_as_children`) asserts the
   controller module exposes exactly one parameter — the decoder layers are
   held as non-owned references precisely so `state_dict()` cannot
   accidentally swallow the base model.
2. **Hot-swappable behavior.** Hooks attach and detach in context managers
   with `finally` blocks; the same model instance can serve base behavior
   and many controllers in interleaved requests.
3. **Auditability.** Every artifact records the model/tokenizer name *and
   revision* it was trained against; loaders refuse mismatched identities by
   default; stores are plain JSON plus `.pt` tensors loadable with
   `weights_only=True` (no pickle execution).

## Component map

```text
                          ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
                          │           frozen HF causal LM              │
                          │  decoder layers (model.layers, ...)        │
                          └────────┬───────────────────────▲───────────┘
                 forward hooks     │                       │ attach/remove
                                   ▼                       │
   ┌──────────────┐   fit    ┌──────────────────┐  apply  ┌┴──────────────────┐
   │ JSONL data   ├─────────►│ SignedLogMask    │◄────────┤ ControllerRuntime │
   │ (data.py)    │          │ controller       │         │ + RuntimePolicy   │
   └──────────────┘          │ (controller.py)  │         │ (runtime.py)      │
                             └───┬──────────┬───┘         └───────▲───────────┘
                  save/load .pt  │          │ s-vector            │ admission
                                 ▼          ▼                     │
                       ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────┴────────┐
                       │ compose.py   │  │ dual.py      │  │ artifacts.py  │
                       │ s_AB = Σwᵢsᵢ │  │ secant/range │  │ lint/card/    │
                       └──────┬───────┘  │ diagnostics  │  │ doctor        │
                              │          └──────────────┘  └───────────────┘
                              ▼
                       ┌──────────────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────┐
                       │ memory.py / retrieval.py │   │ isr.py           │
                       │ store + search + compose │   │ q_marg, ISR,     │
                       └──────────────────────────┘   │ KV order-debias  │
                                                      └──────────────────┘
```

## Three data flows

**Train.** `load_jsonl_examples` parses prompt/completion (or
instruction/response, question/answer, text, or chat-`messages`) rows;
prompt tokens are masked with label `-100`, completions are teacher-forced
(`data.py`). `ForwardFineTuner.fit` first *selects* gates by saliency on up
to `score_batches` (default 16) batches, then runs AdamW (default 240 steps,
lr $5\times10^{-3}$) on the single `raw` parameter, with optional
validation-based early stopping, retain-set loss, and KL-to-base
regularization. `save()` writes a `SignedLogMaskState` dict with schema
version, gate indices, and full model/tokenizer identity.

**Serve.** `ControllerRuntime.apply(...)` validates a state against the live
model (layer path, layer count, hidden size, identity), builds a transient
hook module, attaches it under a lock, yields, and removes hooks in
`finally`. Composition happens at request time from one or more artifacts;
heterogeneous per-row controllers in one batch are rejected rather than
silently mis-served.

**Research.** `secant`, `dual-diagnose`, and `fit-dual` treat the gates as
an activation-control basis and interrogate its geometry against full-weight
SGD fields (@sec-dual); `isr-auc` runs the verifier study (@sec-isr); the
benchmark suite trains one memory per task and evaluates the five-condition
design matrix (@sec-benchmark).

## The prime invariant (H0)

Everything rests on the claim that **the base model is never modified**.
The repo treats this not as an assumption but as a standing, tested
invariant: the controller holds layers as non-owned references; the dual
target's finite-difference weight step is applied and then *reversed inside
a `finally`-guarded block* (`full_weight_sgd_field_finite_difference`
re-adds the gradient step even on exception); `evaluate_nll(...,
use_controller=False)` temporarily detaches and then restores an outer
controller; and runtime hooks are removed even when generation raises
(`test_runtime_apply_removes_hooks_after_exception`).

# The scientific method, reconstructed {#sec-method}

Reading `docs/` and the test suite together, the repository's working style
follows one repeated template:

> **Hypothesis** (a mechanism should work) → **Instrumentation** (a command
> that measures it) → **Diagnostics** (named quantities with thresholds) →
> **Failure modes** (documented ways the measurement can mislead).

The documentation is unusually explicit about the last step: `docs/method.md`
has "Failure modes to check before believing a result";
`docs/activation_control_ntk.md` has "Failure modes to check";
`docs/composability.md` has "Failure hypotheses";
`docs/persistent_memory.md` has "Failure hypotheses to test";
`docs/public_memory_benchmark.md` has "Failure modes directly tested". These
are not disclaimers — each names the *instrument* that would detect the
failure.

## Hypothesis ledger

| # | Hypothesis | Instrument | Refutation signal | Location |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H0 | The base model is never modified; all interventions are reversible | parameter registration, `finally`-guarded hook removal & FD-step reversal | base outputs change after detach; extra parameters appear | `controller.py`, `dual.py`, `tests/test_controller.py`, `tests/test_runtime.py` |
| H1 | A sparse multiplicative log-gate on decoder channels can lower task NLL | `ntkmirror fit` + `eval` | no NLL/token-accuracy gain vs. base across a `--gates` sweep | `controller.py`, `cli.py` |
| H2 | Gates chosen by $\lvert\partial L/\partial s\rvert$ at $s=0$ are the useful ones | `_score_gates` saliency; random-gate control prescribed in README | random gates match scored gates | `controller.py`, README "Notes for benchmark claims" |
| H3 | The trained controller is explained by its initial tangent | `ntkmirror secant` | high `secant_err` at $\alpha=1$ | `dual.py` |
| H4 | The full-weight SGD projected-logit field lies in the gate-tangent range | `ntkmirror dual-diagnose` | high `range_residual` with clean `adjoint_error`/`symmetry_error`/CG | `dual.py`, `docs/activation_control_ntk.md` |
| H5 | Controllers compose additively in log-gate space | `compose`, `compose-plan`, `inspect` | saturation, opposing-sign overlap, negative transfer on held-out evals | `compose.py`, `docs/composability.md` |
| H6 | Controllers work as retrievable persistent memories | memory store + retriever + benchmark suite | low retrieval recall; wrong-memory harm; leakage | `memory.py`, `retrieval.py`, benchmark CSVs |
| H7 | Order-sensitivity of a verifier flags unsupported claims | `ntkmirror isr-auc` | AUROC ≈ 0.5; ISR worse than `q_marg`; confident-wrong verifier | `isr.py`, `docs/isr_kv_verifier.md` |
| H8 | Serving is safe under admission + isolation | `RuntimePolicy`, `lint`, `card`, `doctor` | identity-mismatch acceptance; hook leakage; cross-request contamination | `runtime.py`, `artifacts.py` |

Two methodological refinements recur often enough to call out:

- **Confusable failures get orthogonal instruments.** A bad composed
  controller could be a retrieval error, gate collision, saturation, or
  prompt mismatch; the benchmark emits a separate CSV for each. A high dual
  residual could be a solver bug, a too-large step, or a genuine range
  failure; `dual.py` separates `adjoint_error`, `symmetry_error`, CG
  convergence, `range_residual`, and `realized_residual` so the error
  *ordering* identifies the culprit (@sec-dual-taxonomy).
- **Certificates are replaced by measurements.** The docs explicitly retire
  gate-norm and clip-fraction as duality certificates ("The controller is
  not certified by being small in its own gate chart") in favor of the
  measured range residual, and retire the v1 secant statement
  ($Pz(s)-Pz(0)\approx B(0)s$) as "only a secant diagnostic".

# Core mechanism: the signed log-gate controller {#sec-controller}

**Hypothesis under test (H1, H2):** a sparse multiplicative rescaling of
decoder-layer channels, selected by first-order saliency, is enough to adapt
a frozen model's behavior on teacher-forced data.

## Intervention and parameterization

Each gate $j$ addresses one (layer $\ell_j$, channel $c_j$) pair. The
trainable parameter is an unconstrained vector $r \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$; the
applied signed log-gate is the bounded reparameterization

$$
s_j \;=\; s_{\max}\,\tanh(r_j), \qquad
h'_{\ell_j,t,c_j} = e^{s_j}\, h_{\ell_j,t,c_j}.
$$

The $\tanh$ box keeps every gate in $(-s_{\max}, s_{\max})$ *by
construction*, so no projection step is needed during optimization; with the
default $s_{\max}=0.05$, each channel's scale stays within
$e^{\pm 0.05} \approx [0.951, 1.051]$. Hooks implement the rescaling at one
of two sites: `layer_output` (default; post-hook on each decoder layer's
output) or `layer_input` (pre-hook on the layer's first positional input).
For exact round-tripping of stored values, the inverse map
$r = \operatorname{atanh}(s/s_{\max})$ is applied with clamping to
$\pm 0.999999$.

## Gate selection by first-order saliency

For a multiplicative gate at $s=0$, the chain rule gives the per-channel
loss sensitivity directly from quantities available in one backward pass:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial s_{\ell,c}}\bigg|_{s=0}
= \sum_{t} \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_{\ell,t,c}}\; h_{\ell,t,c}.
$$

`_score_gates` captures decoder-layer activations with `retain_grad()`,
backpropagates the standard causal cross-entropy, and accumulates a
per-channel score over up to `score_batches` batches. The top
`gates` (default 5000) layer–channel pairs by score become the controller
support; their gate values start at $s=0$ (identity behavior).

::: {.callout-note}
## Code vs. docs nuance: $\sum_t \lvert g_t h_t \rvert$, not $\lvert \sum_t g_t h_t \rvert$

`docs/method.md` writes the selection score as the magnitude of the summed
derivative. The implementation
(`(h.grad * h).abs().sum(dim=(0, 1))` in `controller.py`) sums the
*absolute* per-position contributions over batch and sequence — an upper
bound on the docs' quantity. The implemented variant cannot miss a channel
whose positive and negative token contributions cancel; it is a deliberate
(or at least more conservative) saliency choice, and worth knowing when
reproducing the selection.
:::

## Training objective

With the controller attached, `fit` minimizes

$$
L \;=\; L_{\text{task}}
\;+\; \lambda_{\text{retain}}\, L_{\text{retain}}
\;+\; \lambda_{\text{KL}}\, \mathrm{KL}\!\left(P_{\text{base}} \,\Vert\, P_{\text{ctrl}}\right)
\;+\; \lambda_{2}\, \overline{s^{2}},
$$

where $L_{\text{task}}$ and $L_{\text{retain}}$ are mean next-token
cross-entropies over supervised positions (labels $\neq -100$), the KL term
is averaged over supervised retain-row positions with base logits computed
with the controller *temporarily removed*, and $\overline{s^2}$ is the mean
squared signed gate. Both regularizers require an explicit retain set
(`--retain`); the optimizer is AdamW on `raw` only. Validation rows trigger
periodic NLL evaluation, early stopping after `early_stop_patience` stale
checks, and best-checkpoint selection on validation NLL.

## Defaults and artifact schema

| Hyperparameter | Default | Role |
|---|---:|---|
| `gates` | 5000 | controller capacity (top-k saliency) |
| `layers` | `all` | candidate decoder layers (`all`, `last`, `last:N`, indices) |
| `max_log_gate` ($s_{\max}$) | 0.05 | per-gate box bound |
| `hook_site` | `layer_output` | intervention site |
| `steps` | 240 | AdamW steps |
| `lr` | $5\times 10^{-3}$ | gate learning rate |
| `batch_size` / `score_batches` | 8 / 16 | training / selection batches |
| `l2` ($\lambda_2$) | $10^{-5}$ | mean-$s^2$ penalty |
| `retain_weight`, `kl_to_base` | 0 / 0 | opt-in retention objectives |

The saved artifact (`SignedLogMaskState`, schema version 2) stores
`layer_path`, `n_layers`, `hidden_size`, rank-1 tensors `layer_indices` /
`channel_indices` / `raw`, `max_log_gate`, `hook_site`,
`theory_version="activation_control"`, the model/tokenizer **names and
revisions**, a creation timestamp, and free-form metadata. Validation
rejects duplicate gate keys, out-of-range indices, non-finite values, and
unknown schema versions; loading uses `torch.load(...,
weights_only=True)` so a controller file cannot execute pickle payloads, and
the legacy pickle path is an explicit `unsafe_legacy_load=True` opt-in.

**Refutation surface.** `docs/method.md` lists the ways a positive result
here can mislead: evaluation contaminated by problems the base model already
solves; capacity confounds (a 512-gate and a 10k-gate controller are
different objects — sweep `--gates`); NLL improving while exact-answer
accuracy does not; over-large `--max-log-gate` hurting generation; and
non-standard decoder stacks needing a layer-path extension. The README's
benchmark notes additionally demand a random-gate control — the direct
refutation of H2.

# NTK dual: can gates imitate weight updates? {#sec-dual}

**Hypothesis under test (H3, H4):** this is the intellectual core of the
repository. The question is not whether gates *help*, but whether the gate
basis is *dual* to full-weight SGD — whether the projected-logit
displacement that a real (infinitesimal) weight step would produce lies in
the range of the gate tangent.

## The target field: measured, not assumed

For support loss gradient $g_S$ and learning rate $\eta$, NTK theory says a
full-weight SGD step displaces calibration logits (to first order) by

$$
d_C^{\theta} \;=\; -\eta\, J_{\theta,C}\, J_{\theta,S}^{\top}\, g_S,
$$

where $J_{\theta,C}$ and $J_{\theta,S}$ are Jacobians of calibration
projections and support loss with respect to weights. The implementation
refuses to trust this formula symbolically. Instead,
`full_weight_sgd_field_finite_difference` *performs the step*: it computes
the support gradient, applies $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta\, g$ in place
(default $\eta = 10^{-5}$, `--target-step-size`), re-evaluates the projected
calibration field, **reverses the step**, and returns the measured
displacement $d = F_C(\theta - \eta g) - F_C(\theta)$. The reversal is
`finally`-guarded so an exception cannot leave the model perturbed (H0).
Cost control comes from `--param-filter` (substring selection, e.g.
`q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj`) and `--max-target-parameters` (a hard guard
before differentiating the whole model).

## Projected-logit coordinates

All fields live in a fixed projection of supervised next-token logits,
built once from a baseline forward pass (`build_logit_projection`):

- `target` (alias `gold`): one teacher-forced coordinate per supervised
  token — cheap, but only tests the teacher-token event direction;
- `topk` (alias `gold_topk`): the teacher token plus the top-`k` (default
  32) *non-teacher* baseline tokens per position. The v1 implementation
  duplicated the gold token inside the event set, silently overweighting it;
  v2 explicitly excludes it from the top-k before concatenation (a recorded,
  fixed instrumentation bug — see `test_topk_projection_does_not_duplicate_gold_token`);
- `full`: all vocabulary coordinates, exact but `n_positions × vocab` large,
  guarded by `max_dim`.

Logits are centered across the vocabulary before gathering (and the `topk`
event set is re-centered internally), so the coordinates measure
distribution shape rather than a constant logit offset.

## The matrix-free, adjoint-consistent solve

At controller state $s_t$, the gate tangent is
$B_C(s_t) = \partial\, (P_C\, z(s_t)) / \partial s$. The duality question is
answered by ridge-projecting the measured target onto
$\operatorname{range}(B)$:

$$
\left(B M^{-1} B^{\top} + \lambda I\right)\alpha = d,
\qquad
u = M^{-1} B^{\top} \alpha,
$$

solved by conjugate gradients (defaults: `ridge` $\lambda = 10^{-4}$,
`cg_iters` 16, `cg_tol` $10^{-5}$). Nothing materializes $B$: $Bv$ is an
exact autograd JVP, $B^{\top}y$ an exact autograd VJP through
`temporary_signed_log_values` (a differentiable override of the applied
gates). Because both directions come from the same autograd graph, the CG
operator $K = BM^{-1}B^\top + \lambda I$ is symmetric positive-definite up
to floating-point error — *and the code verifies this instead of assuming
it*, with random-probe checks
$\langle Bv, y\rangle \overset{?}{=} \langle v, B^{\top}y\rangle$
(`adjoint_error`) and $a^{\top}Kb \overset{?}{=} b^{\top}Ka$
(`symmetry_error`). A finite-difference JVP (`--jvp-mode fd`) is retained
*only* as an ablation; the docs state plainly that mixing FD forward with
autograd adjoint makes $K$ non-adjoint, so its residuals "are not
reachability evidence."

The metric $M$ is `identity` (an ablation, per the docs) or `activation`: a
diagonal metric $M_j \approx \mathbb{E}[h_j^2]\, e^{2 s_j}$ estimated on the
calibration batch, so that gate displacement is measured in units of induced
activation displacement. By default the implementation records activations
with the controller attached (`record_current_state=True`), which folds the
$e^{2s_j}$ factor in empirically and lets upstream gates influence
downstream metrics; the closed-form $e^{2s_j}$ correction is the documented
approximation recovered with `record_current_state=False`.

## Residual taxonomy {#sec-dual-taxonomy}

The solver refuses to compress the answer into one number. It reports:

| Diagnostic | Definition | What a high value means |
|---|---|---|
| `adjoint_error` | probe gap $\langle Bv,y\rangle$ vs $\langle v,B^\top y\rangle$ | JVP/VJP inconsistent → solver invalid, residuals meaningless |
| `symmetry_error` | probe gap $a^\top K b$ vs $b^\top K a$ | CG operator not symmetric → same |
| `cg.converged`, `residual_history` | CG convergence record | under-solve; raise `--cg-iters` before interpreting |
| `range_residual` | $\lVert d - Bu\rVert / \lVert d\rVert$ | target field not in local gate-tangent range (**the duality verdict**) |
| `realized_residual` | $\lVert d - (F(s+u)-F(s))\rVert / \lVert d\rVert$ | linear range fine but step too large / chart too curved |
| `clipped_realized_residual` | same, after box-clipping $s+u$ | what the *deployable* clipped update actually realizes |
| `field_residual` | alias of `clipped_realized_residual` | safety-facing name for the same number |
| `box_clip_fraction`, `box_active_fraction`, `box_clip_update_residual` | how much of $u$ the box constraint changed | solved step ≠ applied step; read clipped numbers |
| `secant_err`, `cos_local_initial` (from `secant`) | trained-controller departure from initial tangent | H3 fails — but see below |

The test suite pins the clean-instrument regime numerically: on a toy model
with near-exact solves, `adjoint_error` $< 10^{-5}$, `symmetry_error`
$< 10^{-5}$, `range_residual` $< 10^{-4}$, `realized_residual`
$< 5\times10^{-3}$ (`tests/test_dual.py`), and a dedicated test constructs a
box-clipped scenario to assert `clipped_realized_residual ≥
realized_residual` with non-zero clip fractions.

## The error-ordering decision tree

The taxonomy supports a diagnosis procedure that the docs spell out as
failure modes; flattened into a decision tree:

```text
adjoint_error or symmetry_error high?
└─ yes → solver invalid (FD/VJP mix? bug?). Fix before reading residuals.
└─ no → CG unconverged?
    └─ yes → raise --cg-iters; an under-solve mimics range failure.
    └─ no → range_residual high?
        └─ yes → genuine range failure: full-weight field is NOT in the
                 selected gate tangent on this projection. (Check the
                 projection isn't too narrow: target may pass while
                 topk/full fail.)
        └─ no → realized_residual high?
            └─ yes → realization failure: step too large or chart too
                     curved (sweep --target-step-size).
            └─ no → box_clip_fraction high?
                └─ yes → interpret clipped_realized_residual, not
                         realized_residual: the applied step was clipped.
                └─ no → local duality holds on this projection.
```

One distinction deserves emphasis because the docs repeat it three times:
**initial-tangent failure is not dual failure.** A finite trained controller
can leave the secant approximation from $s=0$ (high `secant_err` at
$\alpha=1$) while every *local* field solve along the path succeeds. The
`secant` command exists to diagnose the v1 linearization story, not to
certify duality; `dual-diagnose` is the range test; and `fit-dual` is the
constructive version — a pathwise optimizer that, at each step (the Python
API defaults to 8 steps; the CLI inherits the shared `--steps` flag, default
240), re-measures the local full-weight target field and applies the
best-realizing gate update (reporting per-step `clip_fraction` and
`clip_update_residual` where the box constraint bit).

**Refutation surface.** Beyond the decision tree: too-large $\eta$ measures
a nonlinear weight move, too-small drowns in numerical noise (sweep it);
`target` projection can flatter the controller while `topk`/`full` refute
it; on 7B-scale models the full-weight target is expensive, so scoped
`--param-filter` runs come first; and `layer_output` vs `layer_input`
hook-site ablations are prescribed for theorem-facing claims.

# Composition: an algebra of skills {#sec-composition}

**Hypothesis under test (H5):** because channel scales multiply in
activation space, addition in signed log-gate space is the natural
composition rule for controllers, and disjoint task controllers should
compose without destructive interference.

## The rule and its preconditions

$$
s_{AB} \;=\; \operatorname{clip}\!\Big(\textstyle\sum_i w_i\, s^{(i)},\;
-s_{\max},\, +s_{\max}\Big),
\qquad h' = e^{s_{AB}} h .
$$

`compose_states` materializes each input as a sparse
$(\ell, c) \mapsto s$ map, takes the weighted sum over the union support,
clips into the (largest input, unless overridden) gate box, and re-encodes
through $\operatorname{atanh}$. Composition is *refused*, not fudged, when
inputs disagree on `hidden_size`, `n_layers`, `layer_path`, `hook_site`, or
`theory_version`; optional identity fields (model/tokenizer name and
revision) must agree on an all-or-none basis — a state with a known
identity cannot be composed with one whose identity is missing
(`_common_optional_identity`). The output records its provenance
(`composition` metadata: input count, weights, gate counts).

## Interference instrumentation

`pair_report` quantifies geometric compatibility for every controller pair:
exact gate overlap and union (Jaccard), the fraction of shared gates with
*opposing signs*, dense gate-vector L2 norms, dot product, and cosine.
`composition_plan` turns these into a conservative pre-flight check with
three warning codes and explicit thresholds:

| Warning | Trigger (default) | Mechanical risk |
|---|---|---|
| `composition-clips` | > 2% of composed gates exceed $s_{\max}$ pre-clip | saturation: the sum is not what gets applied |
| `opposing-overlap` | > 50% of shared gates have opposite signs | gate collision: tasks fight over channels |
| `negative-gate-cosine` | pairwise cosine < −0.1 | globally antagonistic directions |

The docs are careful about epistemic status: these are "mechanical risk
signals only; they do not prove behavioral compatibility." The final test
is always behavioral — the composed controller must retain each task's
held-out NLL improvement (`scripts/run_disjoint_composition.sh` trains
GSM8K-style and MBPP-style controllers and evaluates base, task-A, task-B,
and composed controllers on both eval sets).

**Refutation surface** (`docs/composability.md`): negative transfer (fix
with asymmetric weights or smaller $s_{\max}$); gate collision (high
overlap + negative cosine); saturation (lower weights or raise gate count —
notably *not* "raise the clip budget"); under-capacity (sweep gates before
interpreting composition); prompt/data format mismatch masquerading as
composition failure (evaluate each controller on both tasks to separate
them); and NLL improving while exact-answer accuracy does not.

# Persistent memory and retrieval {#sec-memory}

**Hypothesis under test (H6):** a controller is small and composable enough
to serve as a *persistent behavioral memory item* — fit one per document,
preference, or skill; store it; retrieve and compose at inference time —
trading prompt tokens for gate state.

## Store mechanics

`ControllerMemoryStore` is deliberately a local, auditable JSON-plus-files
store:

```text
root/index.json                                  (atomic os.replace writes)
root/controllers/<namespace>/<id>.v<version>.pt  (immutable per version)
```

Lifecycle semantics live in the index rows (`MemoryItem`): namespaces,
monotonically increasing versions (an overwrite *retires* the active row
with `retired_by_version` rather than deleting it), soft delete
(`deleted_at` timestamps, excluded from search), rollback (copies an old
version forward as a *new* version, preserving history), and hard delete.
`audit` checks the whole store: duplicate active ids, missing or unloadable
controller files, orphan `.pt` files, and mixed model/tokenizer identities
within one store. Two security properties are enforced at read time: every
controller path must stay under `controllers/` inside the store root
(path-traversal rejection, tested by
`test_memory_index_rejects_path_traversal`), and every artifact must parse
as a valid `SignedLogMaskState` before it is admitted.

## Retrieval and composition weighting

The store's built-in scorer is a dependency-free TF-IDF cosine over a small
tokenizer with stop-word filtering; `min_score` defaults to a *positive*
$10^{-8}$, so zero-overlap memories are never returned and a no-hit query
falls back to the base model rather than attaching an arbitrary controller
(an explicit design point in `docs/v2_operating_layer.md`).
`retrieval.py` adds two stronger retrievers for the benchmark:

- `embedding`: sentence-transformers cosine
  (default `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`, normalized embeddings);
- `hybrid` (default in the benchmark): min–max-normalize lexical and
  embedding scores per query, then mix with
  $\alpha\,\text{emb} + (1-\alpha)\,\text{lex}$, $\alpha = 0.65$. A
  documented edge case: when all candidates tie (e.g., a single positive
  match), normalization preserves the positive signal instead of zeroing it
  out, which would otherwise spuriously trigger the no-hit fallback.

Retrieved hits are converted to composition weights by softmax over scores
with temperature $T = 0.25$ (alternatives: `score`-proportional or
`uniform`), so composing the top-$k$ memories interpolates rather than sums
uncontrolledly; `compose_for_query` then reuses the @sec-composition
machinery unchanged.

## Scope discipline

The docs draw a sharp boundary: controller memories are for *behavior* —
procedures, style, preferences, local terminology — and explicitly **not**
a replacement for factual retrieval. "If the answer requires a quote or a
date, put that evidence in the prompt." The controller changes forward
dynamics; it cannot insert missing strings into context. The same boundary
appears in the controller card's default limitations text and in the
README's persistent-memory section ("not as a substitute for factual
retrieval, source provenance, or RAG").

**Refutation surface** (`docs/persistent_memory.md`): retrieval error
(inspect `memory search` before blaming the controller); composition
interference (use `inspect`, reduce `top_k`); saturation; undertrained
memories that retrieve correctly but inject weak controllers;
prompt/controller mismatch (facts belong in context); stale or *poisoned*
memories — a controller is "executable-adjacent model state", so stores
must stay trusted, versioned, and deletable; and task non-orthogonality.

# ISR: order-sensitivity as a hallucination signal {#sec-isr}

**Hypothesis under test (H7):** a verifier's support probability for a
*well-supported* claim should be stable under permutations of the evidence
order; instability is a signal that the verifier is keying on position
rather than content. Penalizing dispersion should therefore improve
hallucination detection.

## Scores

Given a claim and evidence spans, the verifier text is a fixed template
(`verbalize`):

```text
Evidence:
- <span A>
- <span B>
Claim: <claim>
Is the claim fully supported by the evidence? Answer YES or NO:
```

The backend returns $P(\text{YES})$ by softmax over the two choice-sequence
log-likelihoods (choices `" YES"` / `" NO"`; an opt-in
`--length-normalize-choices` flag divides every choice's log-likelihood by
its token count, which the docs recommend when choices tokenize to
different lengths). With `num_orderings` $= m$ (default 6,
the first always canonical) and per-ordering probabilities $q_1,\dots,q_m$:

$$
q_{\text{canon}} = q_1, \qquad
q_{\text{marg}} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_i q_i, \qquad
\mathrm{ISR} = q_{\text{marg}} - \beta\,\sigma(q),
$$

with dispersion penalty $\beta$ (default 1.0) and population standard
deviation $\sigma$. Permutations are drawn from a deterministic per-row RNG
(`seed · 100003 + row_index`), so canonical scoring and summaries are
reproducible (and tested to be). `q_marg` is the headline AUROC score; ISR
is the order-stability *gate* score.

A throughput detail with scientific value: `p_yes_batch` scores every
(ordering, choice) branch in fused right-padded batches and is documented —
and engineered — to be **bit-identical on CPU** to the one-sequence-at-a-time
path, because for a causal LM with right padding and an attention mask the
real-position logits are unchanged. The optimization is admissible because
it provably does not move the measurement.

## Optional KV order-debias (`q_kv`)

The optional fourth score reuses the repository's signature move — local
linearization plus ridge projection (@sec-dual) — on a different control
surface: the *value cache*. With the legacy `kv_delta_bayes_ntk` backend, it
prefills the evidence/claim cache, builds a dense value-cache controller
spec over `use_layers` (default 4) cache layers, computes the Jacobian
$B$ of *centered* YES/NO event logits with respect to those controller
coordinates, and solves

$$
\min_q\; \lVert Bq - d\rVert^2 + \rho\,\lVert q\rVert^2,
\qquad
\alpha = (BB^{\top} + \rho I)^{-1} d,\;\; q = B^{\top}\alpha,
$$

(local solver in `isr.py`, $\rho$ = `--kv-ridge`, default $10^{-3}$),
where $d$ steers the event logits toward the order-marginal target
$[\,\bar q,\, 1-\bar q\,]$ in centered log-probability coordinates. The
resulting delta is norm-clipped (`--kv-max-norm`, default 10) before
re-scoring, and the result records `delta_norm`, `delta_clipped`, the
event/controller dimensions, and the clip budget. KV failures are row-local
by default
(`kv_status="error"`), so one cache-layout failure cannot destroy a long
sharded run's non-KV scores.

## Evaluation protocol

`run_isr_auc` evaluates all scores as rankers of supported (1) vs
unsupported (0) rows: AUROC via average ranks (tie-aware Mann–Whitney form)
and TPR at a fixed false-positive budget (`tpr_at_leak`, default leak 0.1,
threshold at the $(1-\text{leak})$ quantile of negative scores). Built-in
datasets cover three regimes — VitaminC (supported/refuted claims; NEI rows
skipped), HotpotQA-distractor (gold-support vs distractor-only rows),
RAGTruth (balanced supported/hallucinated responses) — plus custom JSONL
manifests with `claim`/`spans`-or-`evidence`/`supported`-or-`label` rows.
Deterministic modulo sharding (`--shard i --nshards n`) supports long runs;
row failures are recorded (`status="error"`) rather than aborting unless
`--fail-fast`. Privacy default: raw claims and evidence are *omitted* from
result JSON unless `--include-raw`.

**Refutation surface** (`docs/isr_kv_verifier.md`): `q_marg` "is not a
factuality proof," only a verifier score under sampled orderings; a
*confidently wrong* verifier defeats ISR (low dispersion, wrong answer);
unequal-length choice tokenizations bias sequence likelihoods (use
`--length-normalize-choices`); the KV solve is local to the current cache
and event projection, with large deltas clipped and disclosed; and built-in
loaders are for reproducible research, not product evals — those should use
explicit manifests with stable ids and dataset hashes.

# Runtime, operations, and safety rails {#sec-runtime}

**Hypothesis under test (H8):** request-scoped controller application can be
made safe on a shared model, given that PyTorch hooks are model-global.

## Request isolation

`ControllerRuntime.apply(...)` is a context manager that loads/validates
state(s), composes if several, builds a fresh hook module, attaches under an
`RLock` (policy `serialize_requests=True` by default — hooks are global, so
concurrent heterogeneous requests on one model are unsafe by construction),
and removes hooks in `finally`. The highest-severity failure mode this
design targets is *cross-request contamination*: one user's controller
affecting another user's request. Consistent with that,
`generate_batch` raises `NotImplementedError` for heterogeneous per-row
controllers instead of silently broadcasting one controller over the batch
— "split the batch by controller."

`RuntimePolicy` is the admission knob set: `require_model_identity` (default
on) with `allow_unknown_identity` (default on — strict mode available),
`max_controllers` per request (default 4), optional `max_gates`, and
`serialize_requests`.

## Artifact governance

Three CLI instruments gate artifacts before they reach a store or registry:

- `lint` — schema validity; missing model/tokenizer revision (warning, or
  error under `--require-revision`); gate saturation (warn when > 25% of
  gates sit within 98% of $s_{\max}$); layer concentration (info when one
  layer holds > 80% of gates); missing eval/safety metadata (info).
- `card` — a Markdown controller card with identity, gate statistics,
  layer histogram, lint findings, and optional eval/safety reports. Its
  default limitations text restates the factual-provenance boundary. The
  docs add: "it is not a safety proof."
- `doctor` — pre-flight model compatibility: detected decoder layer path
  (five known paths plus a largest-`ModuleList` fallback), hidden size,
  tokenizer pad/EOS and chat-template status, device/dtype, supported hook
  sites.

Data-layer guardrails follow the same philosophy of failing loudly:
`ensure_pad_token` reuses EOS but *refuses* to invent a new pad token
(embedding resize would be a hidden model edit — an H0 violation);
truncation keeps the right edge where completions sit and errors if no
supervised token survives; chat rows train only on the final assistant turn
by default, with template use explicit (`--chat-template auto|none`).

## CLI as instrument panel

| Command | Role in the method |
|---|---|
| `fit` / `eval` / `generate` / `demo` | deployable path (H1) |
| `fit-dual` | pathwise field-locked training (H4, constructive) |
| `secant` | initial-tangent diagnostic (H3) |
| `dual-diagnose` | range test for duality (H4) |
| `compose` / `compose-plan` / `inspect` | composition algebra + pre-flight (H5) |
| `memory add/list/delete/rollback/audit/search/compose/generate/eval` | persistent memory lifecycle (H6) |
| `isr-auc` | verifier study (H7) |
| `lint` / `card` / `doctor` | artifact and model admission (H8) |

Reproducibility and supply-chain defaults are uniform: models load with
`trust_remote_code=False` unless explicitly overridden; `--revision` /
`--tokenizer-revision` pin checkpoints; and the ISR adapter *raises* if the
installed legacy backend silently ignores requested reproducibility flags
rather than dropping them.

# Benchmark methodology (not results) {#sec-benchmark}

::: {.callout-important}
The benchmark suite defines a *protocol*. The repository contains no
committed run outputs; everything below describes what would be measured
and which artifact would refute which claim.
:::

`run_persistent_memory_benchmark.sh` /
`scripts/benchmark_persistent_memory_suite.py` trains one controller-backed
memory per task (defaults: GSM8K, MBPP, HellaSwag, ARC-Easy, BoolQ; the
paper-style small table uses `TASKS=arithmetic,gsm8k,mbpp`) and evaluates a
five-condition design matrix per task:

| Condition | What it isolates |
|---|---|
| `base` | the frozen model alone (floor; also the contamination check) |
| `gold_memory` | controller quality with retrieval oracle-ed away |
| `retrieved_descriptor_memory` | retrieval from task descriptors (diagnostic only) |
| `retrieved_query_memory` | the realistic per-example retrieval path |
| `composed_all_memories` | interference cost of composing everything |

Comparing adjacent rows separates the two main failure axes: gold vs
retrieved isolates *retrieval* error; base vs gold isolates *controller*
error; retrieved vs composed isolates *composition* error. Metrics are
teacher-forced NLL, token accuracy, and (for multiple-choice tasks)
length-normalized choice accuracy, with summed-loss accuracy disclosed
alongside so length bias stays visible.

The suite's distinguishing feature is that the refutation controls are
emitted as first-class outputs, one file per failure hypothesis:

| Output | Failure hypothesis it tests |
|---|---|
| `cross_task_nll.csv` (every memory × every task) | wrong-memory harm / selectivity |
| `selectivity_summary.csv` | gold-vs-wrong margins, worst wrong-memory harm |
| `retrieval_recall.csv`, `retrieval_sweep.csv` (k = 1..5) | retrieval as the bottleneck |
| `retrieval_method_comparison.csv` (lexical vs embedding vs hybrid) | descriptor lexical failure |
| `composition_report.json`, `controller_pairwise_geometry.csv` | gate collision / interference |
| `controller_diagnostics.csv` (norm, saturation, active gates) | saturation of the gate budget |
| `leakage_audit.csv` (exact + 5-gram train/eval overlap) | contamination of the headline gains |
| `per_example_retrieved_memory.csv` | per-row retrieval audit trail |

`MEMORY_TEXT_MODE=descriptor_prompts` exists because of a *previously
observed* retrieval failure mode recorded in the docs: GSM8K/BoolQ queries
do not contain descriptor words like "grade-school" or "yes-no", so purely
lexical descriptor retrieval fails for reasons unrelated to controller
quality; storing prompt snippets alongside descriptors and comparing
retrievers separates the two. The README's "Notes for benchmark claims"
completes the protocol: always report base, controller, LoRA/SFT baseline,
random-gate control, wrong-memory control, and no-retrieval fallback on the
same manifest, with model/tokenizer revisions, dataset hashes, adaptation
time, and peak memory.

# Discussion {#sec-discussion}

## Recurring methodological moves

Four habits recur across every subsystem and constitute the repository's
"scientific method":

1. **Measure the ground truth instead of assuming the theory.** The NTK
   target field is produced by actually stepping the weights and reversing
   the step; the activation metric is recorded from live activations; the
   fused ISR batcher is justified by bit-identity with the naive path, not
   by an argument.
2. **Give confusable failures orthogonal instruments.** Adjoint vs range vs
   realization vs clipping in the dual; retrieval vs controller vs
   composition in the benchmark; canonical vs marginal vs dispersion in
   ISR. Single-number summaries are systematically refused.
3. **Pre-flight checks before attachment.** `compose-plan` before
   composing, `lint`/`doctor` before serving, store `audit` before
   trusting, projection `max_dim` and `max_target_parameters` guards before
   expensive computation.
4. **Refutation thresholds as standing tests.** The tolerances that make a
   diagnostic "clean" (`adjoint < 1e-5`, `range < 1e-4`, …) live in CI, not
   in prose; clipped-vs-unclipped ordering is asserted; security
   properties (path traversal, pickle loading, identity mismatch) are
   tested as invariants. See Appendix B.

There is also a visible *history of corrected instruments*, which is itself
evidence the diagnostics are load-bearing: the v1 top-k projection
double-counted the gold token (fixed and tested); the v1
"local-controller" secant statement was demoted to a diagnostic; gate-norm
and clip-fraction were retired as duality certificates; the diffusion gate's
`exp` damping was replaced by `cosh` after a documented failure analysis
(Appendix A).

## Honest limitations

- **No committed empirical results.** Every quantitative claim a reader
  might want — does `fit` beat LoRA at matched budget? what AUROC does ISR
  reach? — requires running the instruments. The repo gives the protocol
  and the controls, not the numbers.
- **Capability boundary.** Controllers steer behavior; they cannot supply
  verbatim facts, citations, or fresh knowledge. The docs say this in three
  places, and the card template repeats it per-artifact.
- **Architecture scope.** Gating assumes a standard decoder stack
  (five known layer paths plus a heuristic fallback); exotic architectures
  need a layer-path extension.
- **Serving scale.** Hook-based application is serialized per model
  instance by default; true heterogeneous per-row serving, LoRA/PEFT
  baseline runners, vector-DB RAG, safetensors migration, and a signed
  controller registry are explicitly named as out-of-scope future
  workstreams in `docs/v2_operating_layer.md`.
- **Dual diagnostics cost.** `dual-diagnose` differentiates model weights;
  on 7B-scale models this is expensive, and the prescribed mitigations
  (small batches, `topk` projection, `--param-filter`) trade coverage for
  feasibility.

# Appendix A: the diffusion scale-gate Adam-M experiment {.appendix #sec-appendix-a}

`scripts/diffusion/train_scale_gate_adam_m.py` is a separate research
harness (diffusion U-Net channels, not LM decoder channels) that is
included mainly as a worked example of the failure-analysis style. The
original uploaded experiment used multiplicative channel gates with an
Adam-preconditioner metric $M_t = \text{base}M \cdot e^{2q}$ and a post-Adam
$1/\sqrt{M_t}$ step. The documented forensic chain:

1. **Observed:** $q \to -\infty$ on some channels — multiplicative gates
   self-prune unused channels (a real signal worth keeping).
2. **Failure 1:** $e^{2q} \to 0$ made $M_t$ zero/non-finite, turning the
   global update cap into a NaN-bypassed no-op.
3. **Failure 2 (the naive fix):** clamping kept dead channels finite, but
   then thousands of tiny-metric coordinates dominated the global M-norm
   cap, converting it into a global learning-rate divider.
4. **Diagnosis:** $e^{2q}$ is *asymmetric* — as $q\to-\infty$ the
   post-Adam step $u/\sqrt{M_t}\to\infty$, so the optimizer amplifies
   negative drift instead of damping it. Per the docs' recorded runs, 3–7%
   of channels ran to $q \approx -40$; a $q_{\max}$ bound cannot fix an
   unbounded lower tail.
5. **Fix:** replace $e^{2q}$ with $\cosh(2q)$ (symmetric damping of
   $|q|\to\infty$; the script's default `--m-damping cosh`), represent dead
   channels by a *finite* `q_prune` floor (default −20) with hard-dead
   channels getting forward scale exactly 0, exclude dead channels from the
   active-update cap, floor the metric
   ($M_{\mathrm{eff}} = \max(M_{\mathrm{actual}}, M_{\mathrm{floor}})$),
   and bound one-step damage with per-coordinate
   `max_delta_q` / `max_delta_b` caps. Global caps default *off*
   (`--tau-q 0 --tau-b 0`) because the best uploaded run had effectively
   bypassed its own cap.

The docs include the comparison table from the uploaded runs (the one place
the repository quotes empirical numbers, with provenance): `cosh` damping
keeps $q \in [-5.06, +3.75]$ (original run; $[-4.70, +3.57]$ for the
replacement script) with $0\%$ of channels beyond $|q|>5$, while `exp`
damping runs to $q \approx -42$ with $19.7\%$ beyond. The failure
modes section closes the loop with seven live checks (e.g., `dead_frac`
→ 100% means the floor/LR is too aggressive; caps incrementing every step
means the cap is again a global LR divider; DINO improving only with hard
pruning must be reported "as structured channel pruning, not as an
NTK-linear dual result").

# Appendix B: test-suite invariant catalog {.appendix #sec-appendix-b}

| Invariant | Test | Hypothesis guarded |
|---|---|---|
| Controller registers exactly one parameter; base layers are not children | `test_controller_does_not_register_base_layers_as_children` | H0 |
| `evaluate_nll(use_controller=False)` restores an outer attached controller | `test_evaluate_nll_use_controller_false_detaches_outer_controller` | H0 |
| Runtime removes hooks even on exception | `test_runtime_apply_removes_hooks_after_exception` | H0, H8 |
| State validation rejects bad indices, NaN/Inf, duplicates, bad schema | `test_state_validation_*`, `test_load_rejects_unsupported_schema` | artifact integrity |
| Hook applies exactly $e^{s}$ scaling for an overridden gate value ($s=0.05$ under a $0.1$ box) | `test_signed_log_override_and_state_backward_compatibility` | H1 mechanics |
| Top-k projection never duplicates the gold token | `test_topk_projection_does_not_duplicate_gold_token`, `test_logit_topk_projection_deduplicates_gold_token` | H4 instrument |
| Exact JVP/VJP solver: adjoint/symmetry < 1e-5, range < 1e-4, realized < 5e-3 | `test_exact_jvp_vjp_projection_solver_has_small_adjoint_and_symmetry_error` | H4 |
| Box-clipped realization is reported and ≥ unconstrained | `test_projection_solver_reports_box_clipped_realization` | H4 honesty |
| CG solves SPD systems to tolerance | `test_cg_solve_spd_system` | H4 solver |
| Metric solve uses $M^{-1}$ and reports metric norm | `test_metric_projection_uses_metric_inverse_solution_shape` | H4 metric |
| Compose sums signed log-gates; preserves hook site/theory/identity; rejects mixed hook sites and partial identity | `test_compose_*` | H5 |
| Pair report exposes overlap | `test_pair_report_has_overlap` | H5 |
| Memory retrieves and composes; zero-overlap returns no hits; overwrite needs explicit flag; path traversal rejected; namespaces/soft-delete/rollback/audit; orphan detection | `test_memory_*` | H6 |
| AUROC uses average ranks for ties; TPR@leak boundary behavior; canonical scoring deterministic; row errors don't abort; ridge solve matches closed form; KV debias works on a fake backend; empty evidence rejected; custom JSONL rows parse; CLI accepts `isr-auc` | `test_isr.py` (9 tests) | H7 |
| Runtime rejects identity mismatch, too many controllers, heterogeneous batches | `test_runtime_*` | H8 |
| Pad token is reused, never invented | `test_ensure_pad_token_refuses_to_add_new_token_without_resize` | H0 (data layer) |
| Chat rows parse without a template | `test_load_jsonl_examples_accepts_chat_messages_without_template` | data contract |
| CLI maps v2 validation/retain flags and keeps the legacy KL alias | `test_cli.py` | interface stability |
| Decoder-layer discovery and layer-spec parsing | `test_layers.py` | architecture scope |
| Lint/card/doctor produce findings and reports | `test_artifacts.py` | H8 |

*Report generated from commit `da17ca3` of the `ntkmirror` repository.
Sources: `src/ntkmirror/*.py`, `docs/*.md`, `tests/*.py`, `scripts/*`,
`README.md`, `pyproject.toml`.*
